title: The Frontier of Economics
body:
The frontier now includes AI-driven productivity, platform competition, and the changing economics of work. Economists are trying to understand which tasks are automated, which skills become more valuable, and which policy settings best support adaptation.

Climate economics is another major frontier. Pricing carbon, financing adaptation, and managing transition risk all require models that capture both physical reality and institutional behaviour.

The discipline is also paying more attention to housing, inequality, and the political economy of reform. In practice, the frontier is less about mathematical novelty and more about applying economic reasoning to hard real-world systems where incentives, power, and uncertainty collide.

That makes economics increasingly interdisciplinary and increasingly relevant.
keyPoints: Climate transition creates new pricing questions
Housing affordability is a structural policy problem
Platform markets challenge traditional competition law
Inequality is now central rather than peripheral
furtherReading: The Economics of Artificial Intelligence — OECD and research literature
The Future of Work — International Labour Organization reports
